dovie_byrd.birth_est_basis: Backward calculation: Dale William Spence Sr. born circa 1934-1935 (confirmed from Beaumont HS 1952 championship records); if Dovie is his mother she would have been born approximately 1905-1920. John A Byrd was born 1868, making him 37-52 at Dovie's probable birth — plausible for a mid-career child. The task prompt states ~1912, which is consistent.
dovie_byrd.death_est: circa 1960-1985, probably Jefferson County (Beaumont) or Houston area, Texas

mother.name: Martha Alice Bedford (per GEDCOM) OR Lillie Byrd (per burial co-location — UNRESOLVED CONFLICT)
mother.conflict_detail: KcaMarco GEDCOM names Martha Alice Bedford as John A Byrd's wife. PeopleLegacy shows Lillie Byrd (1877-1948) buried at same Clute cemetery as probable son Otha Lee Byrd Sr. (1902-1989) — the two names do not match. John A Byrd may have had two wives, GEDCOM may be wrong, or Lillie is a sister/collateral figure. See 64-byrd-confirmed-trace.json for full analysis.
mother.resolution: UNRESOLVED — 1910 Milam County census listing John A Byrd's household would resolve this immediately

dovie_byrd.maiden_name_confidence: PROBABLE — family tradition; no primary source document found in open-web research naming Dovie's maiden name
dovie_byrd.notes: Dovie A. Byrd married William Spence (born 1908 Philadelphia PA, son of Joseph C. Spence). Their son Dr. Dale William Spence Sr. was born circa 1934-1935 in Beaumont, Jefferson County, Texas (confirmed public records). No Texas death certificate, marriage record, or census entry for Dovie has been located in open-web sources. Her Texas death certificate (estimated death 1960-1985) is the single highest-priority document remaining.

john_archie_asner_byrd.birthplace: Birmingham, Alabama (per Ancestry genealogical records — PROBABLE); some sources list birthplace as Texas — small discrepancy, exact date consistent
john_archie_asner_byrd.burial: Sharp Cemetery, Sharp, Milam County, Texas

father.name: William Leander Byrd
father.source: Ancestry genealogical records (two independent search returns); KcaMarco 4-GEDCOM convergence; cousin GEDCOM tree 11 (lineage-tree-multi.json) names him explicitly as father of John A Byrd

mother.name: Margarete Rhetta Peradeau (alt. spellings: Perideaux, Perydore, Perrydore, Peradeau)
mother.source: Two independent Ancestry records; cousin GEDCOM tree 11; Margaret Byrd (1829-1925) buried at Sharp Cemetery is PROBABLE identification of this person
mother.huguenot_note: Peradeau/Perideaux is a documented French Huguenot surname. Huguenot families fleeing France after the 1685 Revocation of the Edict of Nantes settled heavily in South Carolina, Georgia, and Alabama. This would give Hunter a distinct French Huguenot ancestry strand on the Byrd matrilineal line.

siblings_probable.location: Franklin, Alabama; later Texas (Maxdale TX per Find A Grave)

research.migration_narrative: John A Byrd was born in Birmingham, Alabama in 1868 — the same year Birmingham was founded as an industrial city. His father William Leander Byrd (1832 Franklin County AL) was a Confederate Army private, consistent with Alabama residency during the Civil War. The family migration from Alabama to Milam County, Texas appears to have occurred circa 1885-1900, part of the documented post-Reconstruction Alabama-to-Texas corridor. Milam County (Sharp community) attracted cotton farmers and small ranchers from the Deep South. John A Byrd died at Sharp in 1928, aged 60. His probable son Otha Lee Byrd Sr. (1902-1989) and probable wife Lillie Byrd (1877-1948) both ended up in Clute, Brazoria County — consistent with the post-1901 Spindletop oil-boom migration from Central Texas to the Gulf Coast industrial corridor (Beaumont, Brazoria Co). Dovie's probable move to Beaumont (Jefferson County) follows the same documented pattern.
research.full_name_note: The middle names 'Archie Asner' appear only in the KcaMarco GEDCOM. The burial index and PeopleLegacy record only 'John A Byrd' — no expansion of the middle initial. The full form 'John Archie Asner Byrd' is unconfirmed by any primary source.
open_questions: No primary source (census, birth record, death record, marriage record) has been located that confirms Dovie as John A Byrd's daughter
The 1910 US Census for Milam County TX would list all children in the John A Byrd household by name and age
John A Byrd's death certificate (August 31, 1928, Milam County) would name his wife — resolving the Martha Alice Bedford vs. Lillie Byrd conflict

extended_byrd_chain.summary: The cousin GEDCOM (tree 11 in lineage-tree-multi.json) extends the chain back to colonial Virginia as follows. All entries from generation 6 and earlier are rated PROBABLE based on GEDCOM convergence; none have been confirmed by primary source in any prior research pass.

chain_from_tree_11.name: Richard Byrd
chain_from_tree_11.confidence: POSSIBLE (cousin GEDCOM tree 11 only — this is a cousin branch, not confirmed as Hunter's direct paternal line ancestor)
chain_from_tree_11.important_note: Richard Byrd (1717-1808) of Westmoreland VA is confirmed as a real historical person in WikiTree, FamilySearch, and Geni. However, he appears in cousin GEDCOM tree 11 — a COLLATERAL branch, not Hunter's direct paternal Byrd line (tree 1). The connection between Richard Byrd 1717 and William Leander Byrd 1832 runs through William John Byrd 1799 (Knox TN) and William BYRD 1775 (Botetourt VA). None of these intermediate generations have been confirmed by primary source.

notable_byrd_connections.name: Richard Byrd (1717-1808) — Westmoreland, Virginia
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: Colonial Virginia planter. Documented in WikiTree, FamilySearch, and Geni as a real historical figure. His son Rev. Richard Byrd (1750-1803) moved to Hawkins County, Tennessee. This is the earliest plausibly-connected ancestor in Hunter's cousin GEDCOM tree 11.
notable_byrd_connections.relation_to_hunter: POSSIBLE collateral ancestor via cousin GEDCOM tree 11 — the genealogical bridge from this Richard Byrd to Hunter's direct line (William Leander Byrd 1832) runs through three unconfirmed intermediate generations
notable_byrd_connections.connection_to_westover_byrds: NOT connected to the famous Westover plantation Byrds. The WikiTree Byrd project and prior research (64-byrd-confirmed-trace.json) confirm that Richard Byrd 1717-1808 of Westmoreland VA is a DISTINCT family from the Westover Byrds of Charles City County. His ancestor William Byrd born circa 1695 is NOT William Byrd II of Westover (born 1674).

notable_byrd_connections.name: William Byrd I (1652-1704) — Westover Plantation, Charles City County, Virginia
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: English-born Virginia colonist. Born in Shadwell, Middlesex, England; father John Bird was a goldsmith with ancestral roots in Brexton/Broxton, Cheshire. Immigrated to Virginia 1669. Inherited Stegge's landholdings; became one of colonial Virginia's wealthiest merchants and planters. Member Virginia Governor's Council 1685-1704. Some of his landholdings became the site of modern Richmond, Virginia.
notable_byrd_connections.relation_to_hunter: NO confirmed connection. The Westover Byrds are a Charles City County (Tidewater) family; Hunter's probable Byrd ancestors trace through the Spotsylvania/Piedmont VA and Alabama corridor — geographically and genealogically distinct. Multiple genealogists, the WikiTree Byrd project, and FamilyTreeDNA Byrd Y-DNA project all confirm that multiple independent Byrd families existed in colonial Virginia. Without Y-DNA testing of Hunter's paternal line, no Westover connection can be claimed.

notable_byrd_connections.name: William Byrd II (1674-1744) — Westover Plantation
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: American planter, diarist, surveyor. Founded Richmond, Virginia (1737). Fellow of the Royal Society of London. Authored the Westover Manuscripts and diaries that are primary sources for colonial Virginia history. One of the wealthiest and most documented colonists in British America.
notable_byrd_connections.relation_to_hunter: NO confirmed connection — same Westover branch as William Byrd I, geographically and genealogically distinct from Hunter's probable Byrd line.

notable_byrd_connections.name: Richard E. Byrd Jr. (1888-1957) — Admiral, Antarctic Explorer
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: US Navy Rear Admiral and pioneering polar aviator. First person claimed to fly over the South Pole (1929) and the North Pole (1926, though disputed). Led multiple Antarctic expeditions including Operation Highjump (1946-1947) and Operation Deep Freeze (1955-1956). Medal of Honor recipient. Discovered Mount Sidley, the largest dormant volcano in Antarctica. Born in Winchester, Virginia.
notable_byrd_connections.family_origin: Father: Richard Evelyn Byrd Sr. (1860-1925), Speaker of the Virginia House of Delegates, born in Austin, Travis County, Texas. Grandfather: Richard E. Byrd (1801-1872), politician in Frederick County, Virginia. Great-grandfather: Thomas Taylor Byrd. Great-great-grandfather: William Byrd who served in the British Army during the American Revolution then moved to northwestern Virginia (Shenandoah Valley). This is a Frederick County/Shenandoah Valley Byrd family — completely distinct from both the Westover Byrds (Charles City County) and Hunter's probable Alabama/Milam County Texas Byrds.
notable_byrd_connections.relation_to_hunter: NO confirmed connection. Admiral Byrd's paternal line traces to the Shenandoah Valley (Frederick/Winchester VA) — a third geographically distinct Virginia Byrd family, separate from both the Westover Byrds and Hunter's probable Spotsylvania/Alabama Byrd ancestors.
notable_byrd_connections.note: Harry F. Byrd Sr. (Governor/Senator 1887-1966) was Richard E. Byrd Jr.'s BROTHER — same father (Richard Evelyn Byrd Sr.). Both Harry the politician and Richard the explorer share the same Shenandoah Valley Byrd ancestry, distinct from Hunter's line.

notable_byrd_connections.name: Harry F. Byrd Sr. (1887-1966) — Virginia Governor and US Senator
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: Virginia Governor 1926-1930; US Senator 1933-1965 (longest-serving Virginia senator). Led the Byrd Organization, the Democratic Party machine that dominated Virginia politics for decades. Newspaper publisher (Winchester Star). Led 'Massive Resistance' to school desegregation after Brown v. Board of Education.
notable_byrd_connections.family_origin: Father Richard Evelyn Byrd Sr. (1860-1925) was Speaker of the Virginia House of Delegates, born in Austin, Texas (family returned to Virginia post-Civil War). Grandfather Richard E. Byrd (1801-1872) was a Frederick County VA politician who owned enslaved people in 1860. Great-great-grandfather William Byrd served in the British Army in the Revolution and settled in northwestern Virginia. This is a Shenandoah Valley / Frederick County VA family.
notable_byrd_connections.relation_to_hunter: NO confirmed connection — same Shenandoah Valley family as Admiral Richard E. Byrd Jr. (they were brothers). Completely separate from Hunter's probable Spotsylvania/Alabama/Texas Byrd line.

notable_byrd_connections.name: Harry F. Byrd Jr. (1914-2013) — US Senator Virginia
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: US Senator from Virginia 1965-1983, succeeding his father Harry Byrd Sr. Ran as Independent after the decline of the Byrd Organization. Same Shenandoah Valley Byrd family as his father.
notable_byrd_connections.relation_to_hunter: NO confirmed connection — same distinct Shenandoah Valley family

notable_byrd_connections.name: John A Byrd (1868-1928) — Sharp Cemetery, Milam County, Texas
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: Pioneer settler of Sharp, Milam County, Texas. Probable father of Dovie Byrd (Hunter's paternal great-grandmother). Part of the post-Reconstruction Alabama-to-Texas migration wave that settled Milam County's farming communities in the 1890s.
notable_byrd_connections.relation_to_hunter: POSSIBLE paternal great-great-grandfather (Hunter ← Dale Jr. ← Dale Sr. ← Dovie ← John A Byrd)
notable_byrd_connections.confidence: PROBABLE as Dovie's father (family tradition + GEDCOM); CONFIRMED burial at Sharp Cemetery

notable_byrd_connections.name: William Leander Byrd (1832-1889) — Frankfort, Franklin County, Alabama
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: Earliest Byrd ancestor with multi-source corroboration in Hunter's probable direct line. Confederate Army private. Father of John A Byrd (1868) per two independent Ancestry genealogical records and cousin GEDCOM convergence. Married Margarete Rhetta Peradeau — a French Huguenot surname.

notable_byrd_connections.name: Margarete Rhetta Peradeau (circa 1829-1925) — Sharp Cemetery, Milam County, Texas
notable_byrd_connections.claim_to_fame: French Huguenot ancestry. Wife of William Leander Byrd. If the identification of 'Margaret Byrd (1829-1925)' buried at Sharp Cemetery with Margarete Rhetta Peradeau is correct, she lived to approximately 96 years of age — migrating from Alabama to Texas after her son John A Byrd settled there. The Peradeau/Perideaux surname is documented in the French Huguenot diaspora communities of South Carolina, Georgia, and Alabama.
notable_byrd_connections.confidence: PROBABLE for the person; POSSIBLE for the Sharp Cemetery identification

three_virginia_byrd_families.summary: There are at least THREE distinct and documented Byrd/Bird family lines in colonial Virginia. Hunter's probable line connects to the Spotsylvania/Piedmont branch. All three are genealogically and geographically separate.

family_1_westover.name: Westover Plantation Byrds — Charles City County, Virginia
family_1_westover.patriarch: William Byrd I (1652-1704), born Shadwell, Middlesex, England; father John Bird, goldsmith, Cheshire ancestry
family_1_westover.descendants_include: William Byrd II (1674-1744), William Byrd III (1728-1777)
family_1_westover.geographic_base: Tidewater Virginia — Charles City County / James River corridor

family_2_shenandoah.name: Shenandoah Valley / Frederick County Byrds — Winchester, Virginia
family_2_shenandoah.patriarch: Unknown William Byrd who served in British Army, settled northwestern Virginia post-Revolution
family_2_shenandoah.descendants_include: Richard E. Byrd (1801-1872), Richard Evelyn Byrd Sr. (1860-1925), Harry F. Byrd Sr. (1887-1966, Governor/Senator), Admiral Richard E. Byrd Jr. (1888-1957, Antarctic explorer), Harry F. Byrd Jr. (1914-2013, Senator)
family_2_shenandoah.geographic_base: Shenandoah Valley — Frederick/Clarke County, Winchester area

family_3_spotsylvania_alabama.name: Spotsylvania / Piedmont Virginia Byrds — Hunter's probable line
family_3_spotsylvania_alabama.patriarch_colonial: Richard Byrd (1717-1808) of Westmoreland, Virginia (POSSIBLE ancestor via cousin GEDCOM tree 11 — not primary-source confirmed)
family_3_spotsylvania_alabama.patriarch_confirmed: William Leander Byrd (1832-1889) of Frankfort, Franklin County, Alabama (PROBABLE earliest confirmed ancestor)
family_3_spotsylvania_alabama.geographic_base: Spotsylvania/Westmoreland/Piedmont VA → Botetourt VA → Knox TN → Franklin County AL → Birmingham AL → Milam County TX → Gulf Coast TX
family_3_spotsylvania_alabama.connection_to_hunter: Hunter's PROBABLE direct paternal Byrd line

confidence_assessment.famous_byrd_connections_verdict: NONE CONFIRMED. Hunter's probable Byrd line is geographically and genealogically distinct from: (1) the Westover Plantation Byrds (William Byrd I/II — Charles City County VA, Cheshire England origin); (2) the Shenandoah Valley Byrds (Harry F. Byrd Sr./Jr., Admiral Richard E. Byrd Jr. — Frederick County/Winchester VA). These are three documented, separate Virginia Byrd families. Without Y-DNA testing of Hunter's Spence paternal line against the Byrd Y-DNA Surname Project at FamilyTreeDNA, no famous Byrd connection can be honestly claimed.

summary_narrative: Hunter's Byrd line runs through his paternal great-grandmother Dovie A. Byrd (born circa 1905-1915, estimated birth year ~1912 per family), who married William Spence. Their son Dr. Dale William Spence Sr. is confirmed in public records (Beaumont HS 1952, Rice University, LSU EdD 1966). Dovie's maiden name Byrd comes from family tradition and GEDCOM convergence; no primary source has been found naming her parents. Her probable father John A Byrd (1868-1928) is confirmed buried at Sharp Cemetery, Milam County, Texas. His parents — William Leander Byrd (born 1832 Frankfort, Franklin County, Alabama) and Margarete Rhetta Peradeau (a documented French Huguenot surname) — are supported by multiple independent genealogical records and are rated PROBABLE. The cousin GEDCOM extends this chain back to Richard Byrd (1717-1808) of Westmoreland, Virginia through four additional POSSIBLE-confidence generations. Hunter's Byrd line is geographically and genealogically DISTINCT from the three most famous Byrd families in American history: the Westover Plantation Byrds (William Byrd I/II — Charles City County VA, Cheshire England origin), the Shenandoah Valley political/explorer Byrds (Harry F. Byrd Sr./Jr. — Senators; Admiral Richard E. Byrd Jr. — Antarctic explorer; all Frederick County/Winchester VA), and the Westover branch. The French Huguenot Peradeau ancestry on the Byrd grandmother's side is the most historically distinctive element currently documented in Hunter's Byrd line. Y-DNA testing of Hunter's paternal line remains the most efficient single step to resolve the Virginia colonial connection question.
